**Brian Little** (born 25 November 1953) is an English [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") manager and former player.

As a player, Little was a [striker](/wiki/Forward_(association_football) "Forward (association football)") who spent his entire career for [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") in a career that spanned from 1971 to 1980. He made 247 league appearances, scoring 60 goals and earning a single cap for [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") in 1975. As a player he won the [Football League Cup](/wiki/EFL_Cup "EFL Cup") on two occasions in 1975 and 1977.

As a manager he has been in charge of [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") and [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington_F.C. "Darlington F.C.") before taking a job in with [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C."), during his tenure he secured promotion to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") by winning the play-offs in 1994. He was then appointed as manager of fellow top flight side Aston Villa where he went on to win the Football League Cup in 1996. He had later spells in charge of [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C."), [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C."), [Hull City](/wiki/Hull_City_A.F.C. "Hull City A.F.C."), [Tranmere Rovers](/wiki/Tranmere_Rovers_F.C. "Tranmere Rovers F.C."), [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham_A.F.C. "Wrexham A.F.C."), [Gainsborough Trinity](/wiki/Gainsborough_Trinity_F.C. "Gainsborough Trinity F.C.") and [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey_official_football_team "Jersey official football team").

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Playing career")]

On leaving school in May 1969, Little signed for [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") who would be relegated to the [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") for the first and only time in their history the following season.

Little played a full season for the Aston Villa Youth side and subsequently made his senior debut on 30 October 1971, in a 4–1 win over [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") in the Third Division at [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park"). He only made two senior appearances that season but was a key player in Villa's [FA Youth Cup](/wiki/FA_Youth_Cup "FA Youth Cup") winning side of 1972. By [1973–74](/wiki/1973%E2%80%9374_in_English_football "1973–74 in English football"), with Villa in the Second Division, he was a regular first team player.[[1]](#cite_note-1) He made 247 appearances for the club and scored 60 goals, and made one full international appearance for [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") in 1975.

He was part of Villa's [League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup") winning teams of 1975 and 1977, scoring two goals in the second replay victory over Everton in the latter final, as well as helping the club climb from the Third to [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") in the early part of the decade, scoring 20 league goals in the [1974–75 season](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_in_English_football "1974–75 in English football") when they were runners-up and clinched promotion to the First Division. His playing career came to a halt in 1980 when he retired at the age of 26 due to a knee injury.

In the previous year, a congenital spinal defect was detected on X-rays taken during a medical examination at [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C."), Aston Villa’s local rivals, resulting in the cancellation of his proposed transfer. He was a forward who formed a prolific partnership with [Andy Gray](/wiki/Andy_Gray_(footballer_born_1955) "Andy Gray (footballer born 1955)"). Little is widely regarded as one of Aston Villa’s greatest players and, in 2007, was named among the 12 inaugural inductees to the [Aston Villa Hall of Fame](/wiki/Aston_Villa_Hall_of_Fame "Aston Villa Hall of Fame").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Coaching and managerial career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Coaching and managerial career")]

Although his playing career was over, Little remained on the Aston Villa payroll as youth team coach. When manager [Tony Barton](/wiki/Tony_Barton_(footballer) "Tony Barton (footballer)") was sacked in the summer of 1984, Little's contract was also terminated and he became first-team coach of [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.").

### Wolverhampton Wanderers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Wolverhampton Wanderers")]

Brian Little was appointed [caretaker manager](/wiki/Caretaker_manager "Caretaker manager") of Wolverhampton Wanderers on 31 August 1986 as successor to [Sammy Chapman](/wiki/Sammy_Chapman "Sammy Chapman"). His appointment came at the end of the blackest spell in the club's history, when three successive relegations had dragged them from the [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") to the [Fourth](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division"). He oversaw a steady start to the [1986–87 season](/wiki/1986%E2%80%9387_in_English_football "1986–87 in English football") before [Graham Turner](/wiki/Graham_Turner "Graham Turner") was appointed manager 36 days later.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Middlesbrough

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Middlesbrough")]

Shortly after leaving Wolverhampton, Little was recruited as a first team coach by [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.") manager [Bruce Rioch](/wiki/Bruce_Rioch "Bruce Rioch"). Like Wolves, Middlesbrough were a financially troubled club and had narrowly escaped bankruptcy. Little was an important part of the club's coaching staff as Middlesbrough's form improved, and with two successive promotions they were in the First Division for the [1988–89](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_in_English_football "1988–89 in English football") season. The season ended in relegation for Middlesbrough but in February Little left the [Ayresome Park](/wiki/Ayresome_Park "Ayresome Park") coaching staff and became manager of [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington_F.C. "Darlington F.C.").

### Darlington

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Darlington")]

Darlington were bottom of the [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") in the [Fourth Division](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division"). He was unable to prevent them from getting relegated to the [Conference](/wiki/National_League_(division) "National League (division)") but they were promoted back into the League at the first time of asking. [1990–91](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_in_English_football "1990–91 in English football") brought more success for Little and Darlington as they won the Fourth Division championship, and by this stage he was on the radar of bigger clubs looking to appoint a new manager.

### Leicester City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Leicester City")]

In June 1991, [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") appointed Little as their replacement for [Gordon Lee](/wiki/Gordon_Lee_(footballer) "Gordon Lee (footballer)"). The Foxes had just avoided relegation to the Third and Little was chosen as the man to turn the club's fortunes around. They emerged as promotion contenders in his first season in charge at [Filbert Street](/wiki/Filbert_Street "Filbert Street").

At the end of 1991–92, Leicester came fourth in the Second Division and qualified for the promotion playoffs, the winners securing a place in the new [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"). They overcame [Cambridge United](/wiki/Cambridge_United_F.C. "Cambridge United F.C.") in the semi-finals and were drawn with [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") in the final. But their promotion hopes were dashed when Blackburn striker [Mike Newell](/wiki/Mike_Newell_(footballer) "Mike Newell (footballer)"), a former Leicester player, scored a penalty which took the [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire") side into the new league. At the end of the [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_in_English_football "1992–93 in English football") season, Leicester qualified for the playoffs again in the new Division One. They overcame [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") in the semi-finals but in the final were 3–0 down shortly after half-time to [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C."). They fought back to bring the scoreline to 3–3, however Swindon scored a controversial late fourth goal from the penalty spot to progress to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League").

In [1993–94](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_in_English_football "1993–94 in English football"), however, Leicester won their third consecutive play-offs with a 2–1 win in the final against East Midlands rivals [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C."). This was Leicester's first win in a Wembley final after seven attempts, and took them back into the top flight after a seven-year absence.

In November 1994, following the dismissal of Aston Villa manager [Ron Atkinson](/wiki/Ron_Atkinson "Ron Atkinson"), Little was linked with a return to [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park") as manager. Leicester, meanwhile, were struggling among the elite and they went on to be relegated in second from bottom place, with just six league wins all season. With Villa yet to appoint a successor to Atkinson, Little resigned as Leicester manager later that month.

Earlier in the year, former Middlesbrough youth coach Little had been linked with a return to the [Teesside](/wiki/Teesside "Teesside") club as manager following the departure of [Lennie Lawrence](/wiki/Lennie_Lawrence "Lennie Lawrence"), but the job went to [Bryan Robson](/wiki/Bryan_Robson "Bryan Robson") instead.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Aston Villa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Aston Villa")]

Within days of quitting Leicester, Little was confirmed as Villa's new manager.
Villa had finished runners-up in the first Premier League 18 months earlier, but were now in the relegation battle. A 1–1 draw with relegated [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") on the last day of the season meant Villa's relegation battle had been won.

Under Little, young players like [Mark Bosnich](/wiki/Mark_Bosnich "Mark Bosnich") (179) and [Ugo Ehiogu](/wiki/Ugo_Ehiogu "Ugo Ehiogu") (237) were now getting more first team chances, but Villa also made a large number of new signings between [November 1994 and August 1995](/wiki/1994-95_avfc "1994-95 avfc"), including [Ian Taylor](/wiki/Ian_Taylor_(footballer,_born_1968) "Ian Taylor (footballer, born 1968)") (233), [Mark Draper](/wiki/Mark_Draper "Mark Draper") (120), [Gary Charles](/wiki/Gary_Charles "Gary Charles") (107) and [Savo Milošević](/wiki/Savo_Milo%C5%A1evi%C4%87 "Savo Milošević") (90). The revamped, younger Villa team gelled well, and [1995–96](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_in_English_football "1995–96 in English football") was a successful season at [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park"). The club finished fourth in the Premiership, reached the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") semi-finals and won the [Football League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup") with a 3–0 win over [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") at [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)"), securing [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") qualification for the fourth time in six seasons.

Villa qualified for the [1996–97](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_in_English_football "1996–97 in English football") [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") and although they were knocked out at the first stage by Swedish side [Helsingborg](/wiki/Helsingborgs_IF "Helsingborgs IF"), they qualified for the [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_in_English_football "1997–98 in English football") competition after finishing fifth in the Premiership. [In February 1998](/wiki/1997-98_avfc "1997-98 avfc"), Little resigned after just over three years as Aston Villa manager, with the club in the bottom half of the Premiership. His successor [John Gregory](/wiki/John_Gregory_(footballer) "John Gregory (footballer)") turned things around, with a seventh place finish clinching a UEFA Cup place.

### Stoke City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Stoke City")]

Little was appointed manager of [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") in May 1998, just after their relegation to Division Two.[[5]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-5) Little arrived with the only objective was to gain an instant return to the First Division in [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Stoke_City_F.C._season "1998–99 Stoke City F.C. season"). Stoke began the season in fine form winning 14 of their first 20 matches and they looked well placed for automatic promotion.[[5]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-5) However their form completely fell away after Christmas and won just seven matches from the remaining 26 and ended up finishing in 8th position. One of those defeats was a 2–0 loss against nine-men [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C."), which Little described as "the worst result in my twelve years in management".[[5]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-5) In July 1999 Little resigned and stated: "I have tried my best and the disappointment is very hard to take. I hope the supporters understand that it's best that I leave".[[5]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-5)

### West Bromwich Albion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: West Bromwich Albion")]

Little made a quick return to management when he was appointed manager of [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C."). The club were languishing in Division One and had been outside the top division since 1986, but continued to struggle in the league under Little.

In January 2000 the promising Italian midfielder [Enzo Maresca](/wiki/Enzo_Maresca "Enzo Maresca") was transferred to [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus "Juventus") for £4 million as Albion battled against relegation. Little was sacked in March 2000 after just eight months in charge and replaced by [Gary Megson](/wiki/Gary_Megson "Gary Megson"), who guided Albion to promotion two years later.

### Hull City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Hull City")]

In April 2000, just one month after leaving [the Hawthorns](/wiki/The_Hawthorns "The Hawthorns"), Little was appointed manager of Division Three strugglers [Hull City](/wiki/Hull_City_A.F.C. "Hull City A.F.C."). The Tigers were hit by huge debts and were locked out of [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park") for one game by the landlord, former tennis player [David Lloyd](/wiki/David_Lloyd_(tennis_player) "David Lloyd (tennis player)"). By the end of the 2000–01 season, Hull had been saved from closure by new owner [Adam Pearson](/wiki/Adam_Pearson_(sports_executive) "Adam Pearson (sports executive)"), and the club's future looked brighter. They reached the Division Three playoffs, but lost to [Leyton Orient](/wiki/Leyton_Orient_F.C. "Leyton Orient F.C.") in the semi-finals.

In autumn 2001, following the dismissal of manager [Peter Taylor](/wiki/Peter_Taylor_(footballer_born_1953) "Peter Taylor (footballer born 1953)"), media reports suggested that Little was going to be approached by [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") about a possible second spell as manager, but the job went to [Dave Bassett](/wiki/Dave_Bassett "Dave Bassett") instead.[[6]](#cite_note-6) By February 2002 Hull City looked well on course for automatic promotion from Division Three. But Little unexpectedly announced his resignation from the club,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and under his successor [Jan Mølby](/wiki/Jan_M%C3%B8lby "Jan Mølby"), the club's fortunes slipped dramatically and they could not even finish high enough for a play-off place.

### Tranmere Rovers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Tranmere Rovers")]

In October 2003, Little made a return to football management with [Tranmere Rovers](/wiki/Tranmere_Rovers_F.C. "Tranmere Rovers F.C."), who were in [Division Two](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

His first season with the [Merseyside](/wiki/Merseyside "Merseyside") club was a success. When he took over they were battling against relegation. But by the end of the season they had climbed up to eighth place and had reached the quarter finals of the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup"). He won the Second Division manager of the month award for April 2004.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In his first full season as manager, he guided Tranmere to a third-place finish in [League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One") but they lost in the playoffs to miss out on promotion.

Tranmere began 2005–06 as League One promotion favourites, but at the turn of 2006 they were facing a relegation battle. The club only avoided relegation with one game to go and, due to the club's poor finishing position in League One, he left the club by mutual consent on 5 May 2006.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Wrexham

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Wrexham")]

Out-of-work Little was linked with a return to management with both [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C.") and [Port Vale](/wiki/Port_Vale_F.C. "Port Vale F.C.").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) However, in November 2007, Little took the reins at [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham_A.F.C. "Wrexham A.F.C."), replacing [Brian Carey](/wiki/Brian_Carey "Brian Carey").[[13]](#cite_note-13) With five games remaining in the season the club were in the relegation zone, seven points off safety. They were relegated to the Conference on 22 April 2008 after a 2–0 loss at Hereford United. However, Little signed a two-year contract, promising to revive the club's fortunes.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The 2008-09 season started well, with a 5–0 home victory against [Stevenage Borough](/wiki/Stevenage_F.C. "Stevenage F.C."), another team expected to challenge for promotion. however, a run of poor results followed, with Wrexham being left in the mid-table battle, only four points above the relegation zone and only keeping two clean sheets all season. Following a 3–0 home defeat against [Rushden & Diamonds](/wiki/Rushden_%26_Diamonds_F.C. "Rushden & Diamonds F.C."), Little left Wrexham by mutual consent.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Gainsborough Trinity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Gainsborough Trinity")]

On 28 August 2009, Little was appointed manager of [Conference North](/wiki/Conference_North "Conference North") side [Gainsborough Trinity](/wiki/Gainsborough_Trinity_F.C. "Gainsborough Trinity F.C."). The club had moved to appoint him following the recent dismissal of manager [Steve Charles](/wiki/Steve_Charles_(footballer) "Steve Charles (footballer)") and the resignation of his coaching staff which included formerly appointed caretaker managers [Dave Reeves](/wiki/Dave_Reeves "Dave Reeves") and [Steve Blatherwick](/wiki/Steve_Blatherwick "Steve Blatherwick"). However, Little would not take charge of the club's away fixture the following day against [Farsley Celtic](/wiki/Farsley_Celtic_F.C. "Farsley Celtic F.C.") after it was announced he would not take over from player/caretaker manager [Adie Moses](/wiki/Adie_Moses "Adie Moses") until 7 September.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Little began building a squad of professionals at Trinity, picking up many players from the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") and other ex-League players from the [Conference National](/wiki/Conference_National "Conference National"), but despite boasting a large squad of experienced players Trinity narrowly avoided relegation to the [Northern Premier League](/wiki/Northern_Premier_League "Northern Premier League") during the 2010–11 season. On 22 August 2011 following one win and two defeats in the opening weeks of the 2011–12 season, Little was sacked as manager of Trinity with [Steve Housham](/wiki/Steve_Housham "Steve Housham") taking over as caretaker manager.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Jersey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brian_Little&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Jersey")]

Little was appointed as the [Jersey FA](/wiki/Jersey_Football_Association "Jersey Football Association")'s Director of Football in November 2014,[[18]](#cite_note-18) overseeing the work of [first team](/wiki/Jersey_official_football_team "Jersey official football team") manager Jimmy Reilly. Reilly went on to lead the islanders to their first [Muratti Vase](/wiki/Muratti_Vase "Muratti Vase") Final victory in four years the following May.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Months later, [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey_official_football_team "Jersey official football team") announced their intention to join [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) and when Reilly stepped aside in early 2016 Little was appointed to replace him.[[21]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-21)

Little won the Muratti in May 2016,[[22]](#cite_note-22) but stepped down as manager the following month and reverted to his position as Director of Football;[[23]](#cite_note-23) allowing him to concentrate on his commitments at [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.").[[24]](#cite_note-24) In January 2024, Little was named by Aston Villa as a member of the *Honorary Anniversary Board* ahead of the club's 150th anniversary season.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
